title: Nuno-felted wrap jacket
subtitle: Merino roving fulled into an open-weave linen base
excerpt: A large nuno-felted piece worked on open-weave linen, then cut into a wrap jacket from a single fulled panel. Merino on linen shrinks 25-35% linearly, so shrinkage calculation is a core skill here. An advanced project for experienced felters comfortable with large-scale work.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from nuno felting techniques developed by Polly Stirling and widely taught in international felting workshops from the 1990s onward. Merino-on-linen shrinkage rates of 25-35% linear are the accepted range across multiple published sources. Jacket-from-panel cutting approach is a standard large-scale nuno method. No single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method where wool fibres are worked into an open-weave fabric base. The fibres migrate through the fabric and felt together on the other side, binding wool and fabric into one integrated textile.
Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.
Fine-stapled wool from Merino sheep. Felts readily and produces a smooth, dense surface.
The second stage of wet felting, where the pre-felted piece is agitated more vigorously to shrink and strengthen the fibres further.
Felting using warm water, soap, and agitation to cause wool fibres to interlock and shrink into a cohesive fabric.
The process of working out how large to cut the base fabric and lay the wool so the finished piece reaches the intended dimensions after fulling.

content.text: Nuno felting is the process of working wool fibres into an open-weave fabric so they migrate through the weave structure and bond on the other side, creating a single integrated textile. On open-weave linen,
merino
roving
bonds well and produces a fabric with a beautifully textured, slightly puckered surface. The jacket is cut from a single
nuno felting
panel, which means you need to plan the finished dimensions before you lay a single wisp of wool.
Shrinkage calculation
is not optional here: it is the core skill that makes this project work.

tools.name: Blocking board (optional, for pinning after fulling)

attrs.title: Test your shrinkage first
attrs.body: Always felt a 30 cm test swatch of the same linen and merino combination before cutting your full panel.

content.text: Linen: open-weave, cut to your pre-shrinkage size (see below). Wool: 200-250 g fine merino
roving
in your chosen colours. You will also need felting soap, hot water (60-65°C), a large flat work surface, and string or pins to mark cutting lines.
Shrinkage calculation
Merino on open-weave linen shrinks 25-35% linearly in both directions. Use the midpoint (30%) for planning unless your test swatch shows otherwise. To find your pre-
fulling

dimensions, divide each desired finished dimension by 0.70. Example: a desired finished panel of 120 cm x 80 cm requires a starting panel of approximately 171 cm x 114 cm. Cut the linen to these pre-shrinkage dimensions, adding 5 cm on each edge for the overlap border where fibres tend to shrink unevenly.
Method

Lay the linen flat on your work surface over two bamboo mats positioned end to end. Lay thin wisps of merino roving over the linen surface in a crosshatch pattern: one thin layer running lengthwise, one running crosswise. The coverage should be light and even; you can see the linen weave through the wool. Over-loading the surface makes
nuno felting
harder: the fibres need room to migrate through the weave.

Wet the panel thoroughly with hot soapy water, pressing gently so all the linen and roving is saturated. Cover with bubble wrap, textured side down, and press firmly with flat hands from the centre outward. Spend 10-15 minutes on this step, working across the entire surface, before attempting to roll. The fibres need to begin migrating through the linen before rolling starts.

Begin rolling. Roll the panel onto one bamboo mat, placing a dry cotton tea towel against the roll to prevent the piece sticking to itself. Roll firmly and evenly, applying steady downward pressure along the mat. Roll 30-40 times, unroll, check that the piece has not folded or creased, re-orient 90 degrees, re-roll and repeat. Continue this cycle for 30-40 minutes, adding hot water as the piece cools.

Check bonding. After 30 minutes of rolling, gently try to lift a small amount of surface roving. If it has bonded into the linen it will resist and the linen beneath will pucker slightly. If it lifts away cleanly, the fibres have not yet migrated sufficiently. Continue rolling and check again every 10 minutes.
Full to finished dimensions. Once bonding is confirmed, begin the more vigorous
fulling

phase. Throw the rolled mat against the work surface repeatedly, alternating with hand-rolling. Alternate hot and cold water rinses every 10-15 minutes. Measure the panel width and length periodically. Stop fulling when the piece reaches your planned finished dimensions.

Rinse and block. Rinse thoroughly until no soap remains. Squeeze gently and roll in dry cotton tea towels to remove excess water. Lay flat and pin to a blocking board if available, stretching the panel to your desired finished dimensions. Leave to dry completely, which may take 24 hours in a cool room.

Cutting the jacket. Once fully dry, mark the jacket pattern on the panel with chalk or pins. The simplest wrap jacket shape is a large rectangle with a curved front neckline and two straight sleeve cuts from the side edges. Cut with sharp dressmaker scissors. Finish all cut edges with a narrow hand-rolled hem or a machine zigzag stitch, because nuno-felted linen can fray at cut edges even after felting.

items.symptom: Wool fibres lifting off the linen rather than migrating through
items.cause: Linen weave is too tight for the fibre to pass through, or the wool layer was too thick
items.fix: Use an open weave with 10-14 threads per cm; fine gauze or open-weave linen works best. Reduce the roving layer so the linen weave is visible through the wool.

items.cause: Rolling was always in the same direction without rotating
items.fix: Rotate the panel 90 degrees after every rolling cycle. Uneven rolling direction causes one axis to shrink faster than the other.

items.symptom: Piece is much smaller than planned
items.cause: Shrinkage was higher than the 30% estimate used for calculation
items.fix: This is why the test swatch step is essential. For future pieces, use your actual swatch shrinkage rate rather than the 30% midpoint.

items.symptom: Surface looks patchy with bare linen areas
items.fix: After the initial pressing stage but before rolling begins, add thin wisps to any visible bare areas and press them in before rolling.

content.text: Care
Hand wash in cool water with a small amount of wool wash. The linen component can tolerate warmer water than pure wool, but cool is safest for the bonded merino. Dry flat; the
wet felting
process is complete and the piece will not shrink further under gentle washing, but prolonged agitation in hot water will cause further
fulling
. Do not tumble dry.
